old_claim.note: Interchangeable Parts provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Interchangeable parts scaled the replacement-part and specification use case for industrial catalogs, but trade catalogs could exist for non-interchangeable goods.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica defines interchangeable parts as identical components that can be substituted for one another and ties mass production of identical parts to nineteenth-century machine-tool industry development.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source reviews interchangeable parts as substitutable manufactured components produced by precision equipment and nineteenth-century machine-tool development.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports a scaling relationship for replacement-component catalogs because substitutable parts make cataloged specifications and ordered replacements useful, while the source does not make catalogs themselves technically dependent on interchangeability.

ontology_before: Industrial Parts Catalogs used a generic enabling edge from Interchangeable Parts, implying interchangeability was required for the existence of trade catalogs.
ontology_after: Industrial Parts Catalogs keeps Interchangeable Parts only as a commercial scaling context for replacement-part and specification catalogs.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to catalogs only for interchangeable replacement parts, not broader industrial trade literature.
A source showed all industrial trade catalogs required interchangeable manufacturing before they could exist.
The interchangeable_parts node were broadened beyond substitutable components and precision manufacturing.
short_reason: Interchangeability scales replacement-part cataloging but is not a hard prerequisite for trade catalogs.
